full_response:
Let the campus be overlaid with the administrator’s “checkerboard’’ whose vertical and horizontal grid lines are at the coordinates  
…, –4, 0, 4, 8, … .  
Each small square therefore has side-length 4; the algorithm you designed for the
library is run separately on the routers that lie inside every such 4 × 4 square, and
afterwards all routers that have been kept by the individual runs are switched on.

Why can this fail?

1.  A router that lies in one square can be closer than 2 m to a router that
    lies in a neighbouring square.

    Example (two adjacent squares)  

        S₁ = [0,4] × [0,4] ,          S₂ = [4,8] × [0,4]

    Put one router r₁ at (3.5, 2) in S₁ and another router r₂ at (4.5, 2) in S₂.  
    The distance between their centres is  

            ‖(3.5,2) – (4.5,2)‖ = 1   <   2 ,

    so their radius-1 coverage circles overlap.

    • Inside S₁ the only router is r₁, so the subroutine will certainly keep r₁.  
    • Inside S₂ the only router is r₂, so the subroutine will certainly keep r₂.

    When the two local answers are “simply combined”, both r₁ and r₂ are left
    switched on, yet their networks overlap, violating the global constraint.

    The problem is that the subroutine optimises only with respect to the
    subgraph induced by the routers inside one 4 × 4 square; it is blind to edges
    (possible overlaps) that cross the square’s boundary.  Nothing in the
    definition of a 4 × 4 square guarantees that two different squares are
    separated by ≥ 2 units.

2.  Even if we subsequently deleted routers that overlap across boundaries,
    the result could still be far from optimal.

    Consider again two adjacent squares S₁ and S₂.  
    Put three routers in S₁ at the points (3.6, 0), (3.6, 2), (3.6, 4) and three
    routers in S₂ at (4.4, 1), (4.4, 3), (4.4, 5).  
    Every router in S₁ overlaps exactly one router in S₂, so in any feasible
    global solution at most three of the six routers can stay on.  
    A routine that maximises inside each square separately will keep all six
    routers, after which at least three must be deleted.  The choice of which
    three to remove requires looking at both squares simultaneously; doing the
    deletion locally may remove more routers than necessary and therefore fail
    to achieve the true optimum.

The essence is that partitioning the campus ignores edges of the unit-disk
intersection graph that run between different 4 × 4 squares.  Because those
edges are exactly what encode the “no-overlap’’ constraint, solving the
sub-instances independently and concatenating the answers is neither guaranteed
to satisfy the constraint nor to maximise the number of active routers.

\boxed{\text{Routers in different 4×4 squares can be closer than 2 units, so treating the squares
independently may select two routers whose coverage disks overlap; hence the
administrator's proposal is incorrect.}}  

Confidence: 77%
